output:
**The answer is (B).**

This is a textbook case of **confirmation bias compounded by a missing counterfactual**. The manager only evaluated candidates they already selected — all from top-10 universities — so they never observed how non-top-10 graduates would have performed. The 5 successful hires don't validate the university filter; they validate the manager's own selection process, which was already biased toward that criterion.

The other options identify real but secondary concerns. (B) identifies the core structural flaw: you can't claim X causes Y when your own biased filtering ensured you only ever tested X.
